1: And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying — the new revelation formula opens the most extensive sexual-ethics legislation in the Torah; Lev 18 is the foundational document of Israelite sexual ethics, framed by the Egypt/Canaan contrast (Leviticus 18:2-5,24-30) and structured around the ervah (nakedness) motif.
2: Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, I am YHWH your God — the divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH Eloheichem) grounds the sexual ethics in covenant identity before any specific prohibition; the motivation for the following legislation is not primarily social utility or natural law but covenant loyalty to the one who brought Israel out of Egypt. The Holiness Code (Leviticus 17–26) opens and closes every major section with variations of this formula.
3: You shall not do as they do in the land of Egypt, where you lived, and you shall not do as they do in the land of Canaan, to which I am bringing you. You shall not walk in their statutes — the negative framing by two geographical-cultural poles: Egypt (the past of slavery) and Canaan (the future of conquest). Both cultures serve as negative examples of sexual practice; the framing of Israelite ethics as a third way between two known systems is structurally similar to the Deuteronomic contrast between Egypt and Canaan (Deuteronomy 11:10; Leviticus 17:16). Egyptian records show no comprehensive sexual prohibition code; Ugaritic texts from Canaan similarly show no equivalent to the Levitical list.
4: You shall follow my rules and keep my statutes and walk in them. I am YHWH your God — the positive counterpart to Leviticus 18:3's negative: instead of Egyptian/Canaanite statutes, Israel is to follow YHWH's mishpatim (judgments) and chuqqim (statutes). The self-identification formula (ani YHWH Eloheichem) repeated from Leviticus 18:2 creates a bracketing emphasis: the entire legislative section hangs on covenant identity.
5: You shall therefore keep my statutes and my rules; if a person does them, he shall live by them (va-chai bahem): I am YHWH — the promise of life through the statutes is one of the most important statements in Leviticus; Paul cites it in Galatians 3:12 and Romans 10:5 as the principle of the law-covenant (life through doing), which he contrasts with faith-righteousness. Ezekiel 20:11,13,21 also cites this verse as the foundational covenant life-promise.
6: None of you shall approach any one of his close relatives to uncover nakedness (lo tiqrevu lechol-she'er besaro legalot ervah). I am YHWH — she'er (flesh-kin) covers blood relatives and relatives by marriage; galot ervah (uncover nakedness) is the chapter's central euphemism for sexual intercourse within prohibited relationships. The proximity of kinship is the defining criterion; the ervah framework defines the family as a protected zone of non-sexual relationships.
7: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father, which is the nakedness of your mother; she is your mother, you shall not uncover her nakedness — the mother is doubly protected: directly (she is your mother) and indirectly (her sexuality belongs to the father). The equivalence 'father's nakedness = mother's nakedness' (Leviticus 18:8 separates these for the stepmother case) indicates that the first prohibition covers the biological mother primarily as the father's wife.
8: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife; it is your father's nakedness — the stepmother case (father's wife who is not biological mother): the prohibition is stated separately, framed as a violation of the father's domain rather than only the woman's personhood. Reuben's violation (Genesis 35:22; Leviticus 49:3-4) is the paradigmatic case; 1 Corinthians 5:1 identifies a Corinthian case of this violation as the reason for Paul's most severe church discipline.
9: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your sister, your father's daughter or your mother's daughter, whether brought up in the family or in another home — the sister prohibition covers full sisters (same father and mother) and half-sisters on either side; the phrase 'brought up in another home' (born outside the household) closes a potential loophole for half-siblings raised separately. Tamar's hope in 2 Samuel 13:13 that David might give her to Amnon in marriage suggests that at some point half-sibling marriage was considered possible (or Tamar was desperate); the Levitical prohibition is absolute.
10: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your son's daughter or of your daughter's daughter, for their nakedness is your own nakedness — grandchild incest is prohibited by derivation: one's grandchild carries one's own flesh (ki ervatekha hennah, their nakedness is your nakedness). The generational extension of kinship ties brings grandchildren under the same protective framework as children.
11: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's wife's daughter, brought up by your father, since she is your sister — stepsister (father's wife's daughter from a previous marriage, not biologically related): prohibited by the family-relationship created by the father-stepmother marriage, even without blood relation. The relational/social kinship created by marriage is treated as equally prohibitive as biological kinship in several of these cases.
12: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's sister; she is your father's near kinswoman — paternal aunt prohibition; the term she'er (flesh-kin, near kinswoman) names the basis for the prohibition. The paternal aunt case appears in the Moses genealogy: Amram married his father's sister Jochebed (Exodus 6:20; Numbers 26:59), a pre-Sinai marriage that the Levitical legislation would prohibit. The law's retrospective inapplicability to Moses's parents is implicit in the Sinai-origin formula of Leviticus 7:38.
13: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister, for she is your mother's near kinswoman — maternal aunt prohibition; symmetrical with Leviticus 18:12. The two aunt prohibitions (paternal and maternal) create comprehensive coverage of the parents' siblings.
14: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your father's brother, that is, you shall not approach his wife; she is your aunt — paternal uncle's wife prohibition (not the uncle himself, as the sexual category concerns the man-woman relationships in this framework); the dodah (aunt, uncle's wife) is the prohibited woman, not the uncle's own person. The affinal (by marriage) kinship is treated as prohibitive alongside consanguineal (by blood) kinship.
15: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your daughter-in-law; she is your son's wife, you shall not uncover her nakedness — daughter-in-law prohibition; the double rationale (she is your daughter-in-law + she is your son's wife) emphasizes both the generational relationship and the specific marital bond. Judah's violation with Tamar (Genesis 38:15-18) — even though done in ignorance — is resolved through a complex genealogical providential narrative; the Levitical law makes the prohibition explicit.
16: You shall not uncover the nakedness of your brother's wife; it is your brother's nakedness — brother's wife prohibition (with the exception of levirate marriage in Deuteronomy 25:5-10 for a childless brother). The Herodias case (Mark 6:17-18; Matthew 14:3-4) — John the Baptist's rebuke of Herod Antipas for marrying his brother's wife Herodias while the brother (Philip) was still living — represents the precise violation of Leviticus 18:16, and John's rebuke draws on this legislation.
17: You shall not uncover the nakedness of a woman and of her daughter, and you shall not take her son's daughter or her daughter's daughter to uncover her nakedness; they are relatives; it is depravity (zimmah hi) — the three-generation prohibition: a woman, her daughter, and her granddaughter may not all be wives of the same man (not simultaneously and not serially). Zimmah (deliberate wickedness, depravity) is the strongest evaluative term in the chapter; this violation receives the burning death penalty in Leviticus 20:14.
18: And you shall not take a woman as a rival wife to her sister, uncovering her nakedness while her sister is still alive — the rival-sisters prohibition (not a permanent ban on marrying a deceased wife's sister, but on having two sisters simultaneously as wives): the Jacob/Leah/Rachel case is the obvious background; the legislation formalizes what the narrative already portrayed as a source of profound family dysfunction. The prohibition targets the tzarar (rivalry, vexation) that polygamous sister-marriages create.
19: You shall not approach a woman to uncover her nakedness while she is in her menstrual uncleanness (niddah) — the niddah prohibition (cf. Leviticus 15:19-24; Leviticus 20:18) placed within the sexual prohibition list connects menstrual impurity to the broader ervah framework; sexual intercourse during niddah uncovers the woman's nakedness in her most vulnerable and ritually restricted state.
20: And you shall not lie sexually with your neighbor's wife and so make yourself unclean with her — adultery (Leviticus 20:10: capital offense for both parties) is briefly stated in the ervah framework; the tum'ah (impurity) consequence for the man is noted here. The brevity of the adultery prohibition relative to the incest prohibitions reflects that adultery was already comprehensively addressed in the Decalogue (Exodus 20:14) and other legislation.
21: You shall not give any of your children to offer them to Molech, and so profane the name of your God: I am YHWH — Molech sacrifice in a sexual-prohibition chapter is contextually anomalous but theologically coherent: both groups of laws concern misuse of the life-generating capacities of the family; Molech-sacrifice is the ultimate perversion of parental relation (giving children to death rather than life), placed at the center of the chapter's list. Molech was a deity associated with child-sacrifice in Ammon and Canaan (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 32:35; 1 Kings 11:7); the practice is attested archaeologically in the topheth sacrificial precinct outside Jerusalem (Gehenna/Ben Hinnom valley).
22: You shall not lie with a male as with a woman (mishkevei isha); it is an abomination (to'evah) — to'evah (abomination, something YHWH detests) is applied to several violations in Lev 18 and 20 (Leviticus 18:26,29,30; Leviticus 20:13); it is not a unique categorical term applied only to this prohibition but a severity marker applied to violations that fall outside YHWH's created order. The mishkevei isha (lyings of a woman) formula specifies the sexual category being prohibited by reference to its female analog.
23: And you shall not lie with any animal and so make yourself unclean with it, neither shall any woman give herself to an animal to lie with it: it is perversion (tevel) — bestiality (Leviticus 20:15-16: capital for both man and woman) is the closing prohibition of the sexual list; tevel (confusion, mixing of kinds, violation of species boundaries) is the evaluative term, the same used in Leviticus 19:19 for hybrid-kind violations (crossbreeding, mixed seed, mixed fabric). The common thread: violations of created categories are tevel; violations of YHWH's sanctity are to'evah.
24: Do not make yourselves unclean by any of these things, for by all these the nations I am driving out before you have made themselves unclean — the closing frame (Leviticus 18:24-30) returns to the Egypt/Canaan contrast of Leviticus 18:1-5; the Canaanite nations' defilement through these practices is the reason YHWH is expelling them. The sexual violations are not merely interpersonal wrongs but land-defiling acts with territorial consequences.
25: And the land became unclean, so that I punished its iniquity upon it, and the land vomited out its inhabitants (vataqe ha-aretz et-yoshveiha) — the land-vomiting metaphor: the land has a quasi-organic response to moral pollution; it cannot contain the accumulated defilement and expels its population as a contaminated stomach expels its contents. This is one of the most theologically distinctive concepts in the Holiness Code; the land is not inert but responsive to the moral quality of its inhabitants' behavior.
26: But you shall keep my statutes and my rules and do none of these abominations, either the native or the stranger who sojourns among you — the covenant counter-command: Israel and its sojourners are to follow YHWH's laws, not the Canaanite practices. The inclusion of the sojourner (ger) in both prohibition and command confirms that the land's holiness requirement applies to all who inhabit it, not only to covenant-Israel.
27: For the people of the land, who were before you, did all of these abominations, so that the land became unclean — the historical basis for the legislation: the Canaanites' sexual violations produced the land's defilement that now requires their expulsion. Israel is being brought into a land that has already been through one cycle of defilement-and-expulsion; the warning is experientially grounded in the history of the land itself.
28: Lest the land vomit you out when you make it unclean, as it vomited out the nation that was before you — the direct warning: the same land-vomiting that expelled the Canaanites will expel Israel if they commit the same violations. The symmetry between Canaan's fate and Israel's potential fate is the chapter's most urgent ethical logic; Israel's election does not exempt them from the land's moral ecology. The prophets later invoke this warning as the theological explanation for the Babylonian exile (Ezekiel 36:17; Amos 2:9-10).
29: For everyone who does any of these abominations, the persons who do them shall be cut off from among their people — the comprehensive karet threat for all the prohibitions listed in Leviticus 18:6-23; the individual penalties specified in Lev 20 (capital vs. karet) refine this general statement, but the basic principle is that any of these violations removes the offender from the covenant community.
30: So keep my charge never to practice any of these abominable customs that were practiced before you, and never to make yourselves unclean by them: I am YHWH your God — the final divine self-identification formula closes the entire chapter with the same formula that opened it (Leviticus 18:2). The bracketing ani YHWH Eloheichem encapsulates the chapter's theological argument: all sexual ethics flows from and returns to the covenant identity. The charge to 'keep my charge' (ushmartem et-mishmarti) is the same expression used for the priestly duty (Numbers 3:28) and for the general covenant obligation (Genesis 26:5; Leviticus 22:9).
